channel.title: Dhaka Tribune - Opinion
channel.description: Op-Eds, Editorials and Longform from Dhaka Tribune

item.title: The Padma Barrage: Ignoring historical warnings
item.description:
Before the barrages, before the embankments, before the Krug Mission and the Flood Action Plan and the Ecnec approvals that now arrive every few decades bearing price tags in the billions -- before any of this, there was the bend.
The Kopai River carves a sharp turn through the Rarh region of western Bengal, a landscape of laterite soil that turns the water rust-red in monsoon. At this bend, which the novelist Tarashankar Bandyopadhyay would later make immortal under the name Hansuli Turn , the Kahar community lived out a relationship with the river that was not merely economic or hydrological but ritual.
The Kahars were an “untouchable” community whose traditional livelihood of palanquin-bearing had collapsed under modernity’s advance. They turned, as so many marginal communities in Bengal have always turned, to the river and the land it nourished. But their agriculture was not the simple matter of planting and harvest. It depended on an embankment -- one they maintained not as the state maintains infrastructure, with budgets and contractors and five-year plans, but as a community maintains a covenant.
The elders walked the bank at dawn, reading the water’s mood. When the monsoon came, they understood that the river must be allowed to spill -- not everywhere, not without guidance, but enough to deposit its gift of silt. This was flood not a disaster but as renewal, negotiated across generations.
Tarashankar’s 1947 novel Hansuli Banker Upakatha -- translated into English as The Tale of Hansuli Turn is built around a fundamental conflict. On one side stand Banwari and Suchand, the elders who maintain the community’s myths and their ritual relationship with the land. The river is understood as a living presence, named for Kopavati, a wrathful woman who can destroy everything during monsoon yet remains fertile with sediment and yields good crops.
On the other side stands Karali, a young Kahar who sees the old ways as chains. He brings railroads, wage labour, brick kilns. He breaks from the community’s stories. And in breaking from the stories, he severs the covenant. The embankment, no longer maintained as ritual, begins its slow decay. The river, no longer negotiated with, becomes an adversary.
Tarashankar was not writing environmental policy. He was writing fiction -- recognized as one of the great novels of modern Bengal. But what his novel captures with the precision that only literature can achieve is what was lost when river management was severed from community practice and handed to external authorities.
The Kahars’ embankment was never merely infrastructure. It was the physical manifestation of a social contract. When Karali chose the railroad over the ritual, he did not simply modernize. He broke a relationship that had sustained life at Hansuli Turn for longer than memory.
The knight and his doubt
What Tarashankar captured in fiction, a knighted British engineer had already spent his final years trying to tell the empire -- and being ignored.
Sir William Willcocks was born in India in 1852, trained at Roorkee Engineering College, and went on to design the first Aswan Dam, completed in 1902. He was knighted for his services to imperial irrigation. He designed the Hindiya Barrage on the Euphrates, bringing 3.5 million acres under cultivation in Mesopotamia.
This was, by any measure, one of the most accomplished civil engineers of the imperial age -- a man who had spent his entire career building precisely the kind of large-scale hydraulic infrastructure that defined colonial development and that the Padma Barrage, approved by Bangladesh’s Ecnec in May 2026, proposes to replicate at a Phase I cost of Tk 33,474 crore.
And yet.
In 1930, near the end of his life, the 78-year-old Willcocks stood before the University of Calcutta and delivered a series of lectures that amounted to an indictment of his own tradition. The lectures, published by the university under the title Lectures on the Ancient System of Irrigation in Bengal and Its Application to Modern Problems , argued that British rule had destroyed a superior indigenous irrigation system in Bengal.
Working alongside the malariologist CA Bentley, Willcocks contended that pre-colonial Bengal had possessed a system of “overflow irrigation” that was admirably suited both to agricultural productivity and to malaria control. The annual flooding that colonial engineers saw as a problem to be controlled was, in fact, the mechanism by which the delta renewed its fertility.
Silt-laden water, guided by community-maintained embankments and allowed to spill onto floodplains in controlled ways, deposited nutrients and raised land levels. Stagnant water bred mosquitoes; flowing, silt-heavy water did not.
The colonial administration had not improved Bengal’s waterscape. It had, Willcocks argued, wrecked it -- replacing a negotiated relationship with the delta’s dynamics with rigid embankments that separated land from water, reduced fertility, and increased disease.
The historian Daniel Klingensmith, in research presented to the American Historical Association in 2011, confirms that Willcocks and Bentley’s ideas “received widespread acceptance.” But the colonial administration could not accept them. To do so would have required admitting that its entire approach to water management in Bengal was not merely imperfect but actively destructive.
The knowledge was institutionally buried. The lectures were published -- a 128-page volume with a folded map, later reprinted in 1984 by BR Publishing Corporation in Delhi -- but the policy did not change. The embankments stayed.
Willcocks died two years after the lectures, in 1932, estranged from the establishment that had knighted him. His obituary in Nature described him as “fond of controversy” and “somewhat intolerant of adverse opinion” -- the language of institutional marginalization rendered as personality flaw -- but conceded that “subsequent events have vindicated many of his opinions.” He died on the margins, vindicated but uncelebrated.
The unbroken logic
What followed Willcocks’s warning was not a change of course but a deepening of the logic he had warned against.
In 1954 and 1955, devastating floods struck East Pakistan. The United Nations dispatched what became known as the Krug Mission, which recommended the “Cordon Approach” -- uninterrupted embankments separating floodplains from rivers. This was precisely the logic Willcocks had argued against.
A Dutch water engineering professor, Willem Johan van Blommenstein, served on the mission. The Dutch came from a delta that carries 2.7 million tons of sediment annually -- a landscape where the cordon approach made a certain sense.
The Bengal delta carries between 1.5 and 2 billion tons. They were solving a problem their rivers did not pose, using methods their landscape could sustain, and imposing them on a delta that works by entirely different rules.
From 1961 to 1964, the Coastal Embankment Project, funded by USAID and inspired by the Dutch polder system, constructed approximately 4,800 kilometres of embankments, creating 139 permanent polders.
The community practices that Willcocks had documented -- and that Tarashankar’s fictional Kahars had lived -- were declared obsolete. Within decades, riverbeds silted up. Land inside polders subsided below river level. Permanent waterlogging began affecting over two million people.
By the 1990s, Dutch experts were deeply involved in Bangladesh’s Flood Action Plan, and from 2012, Dutch-Bangladesh water cooperation officially shifted “from traditional aid to sustainable trade,” focusing on “economic cooperation, investment, and trade promotion.” The cordon approach persisted under new branding. The warning Willcocks had delivered in 1930 remained unheeded.
Then, in the 1990s, something remarkable happened in the southwest delta. Communities facing permanent waterlogging did what their ancestors would have done, though the knowledge had to be painfully reconstructed. They broke open a polder embankment.
The state sued them for destroying government property. But the tidal water rushed in, deposited sediment, raised the land -- and the waterlogging receded. Tidal River Management, the very system Willcocks had described in 1930, was rediscovered through an act of civil disobedience. It was later recognized in the Bangladesh Delta Plan 2100, though it remains chronically underfunded.
And now, 2026 : The Padma Barrage has been approved. A 2.1-kilometre structure with 78 spillway gates, 18 undersluices, two fish passes, a navigation lock, and a railway bridge. Storage of 2,900 million cubic metres -- roughly 3 BCM -- on a river that carries between 350 and 525 BCM annually.
The promised irrigation for 1.9 million hectares would require, by the calculations of water expert Professor Md Khalequzzaman, between 9 and 26 BCM of water. The math does not work.
The sediment, estimated at 400 to 600 million tons annually, will be trapped. The delta will starve. The warnings Willcocks delivered in 1930 are being ignored again -- this time not by colonial administrators but by our own.
The man who became a myth
There is another figure in this story, one who lives where the failure of taming the river has already unfolded.
In Syed Mustafa Siraj’s Sahitya Akademi Award-winning novel Aleek Manush , set in Murshidabad district on the banks of the Padma, the protagonist Safi is a rebel who rejects the state, which he views as a “torture machine” run by complicit officers and state-owned “hooligans.”
The Farakka Barrage looms over this landscape -- the original wound, the structure that slashed dry-season flows and began the saline advance into the Sundarbans.
Safi, wandering the eroded banks, becomes a mythical figure to both Hindus and Muslims. The novel’s title, Aleek Manush , means “The Unreal Man” -- an irony the text itself insists upon, for Safi is very much flesh and blood, very much real. What is unreal, the novel suggests, is the state’s claim to legitimacy, its promise that structures like Farakka serve the people.
Siraj, who spent his youth traveling rural West Bengal with the folk theatre group Aalkaap, brought to his writing what the poet-journalist Syed Hasmat Jalal called “an unparalleled creative genius.”
The novelist Sunil Gangopadhyay observed that Siraj depicted rural Bengal “with far more authenticity than writers like me who live in the city.” And Siraj himself was explicit about his philosophical commitments: “I am free of religious belief,” he said, but also “tolerant towards anybody’s religion.”
His deeper conviction was that the state, particularly in its relationship with nature and rural communities, was a mechanism of violence. “Man tries to control nature,” he observed, “as for example building a dam across a river.”
The violence of not listening
There is a theoretical name for what has happened here.
The scholar Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak, in her foundational essay “ Can the Subaltern Speak? ”, argued that marginalized communities are structurally silenced not because they lack voice, but because the very framework of institutional expertise renders their knowledge inaudible. The subaltern speaks. The powerful cannot hear -- not out of malice alone, but because the structure of power determines what counts as knowledge and whose knowledge counts.
This is the arc that connects Willcocks in 1930 to the Ecnec approval in 2026.
Willcocks, unusually for a colonial engineer -- perhaps uniquely for a knighted designer of the Aswan Dam -- tried to hear what the delta and its people were saying. He recognized that the overflow irrigation system was not primitive but sophisticated, not superstition but science of a different order. He was ignored. His ideas were institutionally buried.
The Krug Mission did not consult the communities whose irrigation systems they dismantled. The Dutch model was imposed without asking whether a low-sediment delta’s solutions could work in the most sediment-rich delta on earth. Tidal River Management emerged only because communities, facing inundation, forced it through direct action -- breaking the law to prove what the law had made unhearable.
And now the Padma Barrage is approved, with claims of irrigation, salinity reduction, and hydropower that the evidence does not support.
The promised irrigation would require three to nine times the water the barrage can store. The sediment trapping will replicate and compound the ecological damage Farakka has inflicted for half a century.
The promised benefits to fisheries ignore that hilsa migration, already disrupted by Farakka, will be obstructed further. No comprehensive, public Environmental Impact Assessment (EIS) has been conducted. The communities whose lives will be reshaped by this structure have not been meaningfully consulted.
The epistemological violence continues. Only the passports of the engineers have changed.
The question the river asked
Picture Sir William Willcocks in Calcutta, 1930.
He is 78 years old. He has built dams across the Nile and the Euphrates. He has been knighted by an empire. He has spent his career in the service of precisely the kind of large-scale hydraulic engineering that the Padma Barrage represents.
And he is standing in a university hall, at the end of his life, telling that empire that the Bengali peasants it dismissed as primitive understood the delta better than its finest engineers.
Willcocks died, estranged from the establishment he had served whose lectures were published, then ignored. The cordon approach rolled on through the Krug Mission, the Coastal Embankment Project, the Flood Action Plan. Farakka was built. The polders were built. The waterlogging came.
The TRM communities had to break the law to prove what Willcocks had told the university 70 years earlier. And now the Padma Barrage proposes to repeat the pattern on an even larger scale.
At Hansuli Turn , the brick kilns that Karali’s logic brought now line the Kopai’s banks. The river Tarashankar immortalized is reduced to a trickle. The embankment the Kahars maintained as ritual has been replaced by an embankment the state built and abandoned. The covenant is broken.
At Murshidabad, on the banks of the Padma, Safi still walks. The Aleek Manush . The unreal man who is more real than the state’s promises. He walks where Farakka’s downstream consequences continue to unfold -- the erosion, the water scarcity, the slow saline creep. He is a figure of resistance against the very logic that now proposes to build another barrage, further downstream, with the same justifications and the same silences.
And Sir William Willcocks, the knight who listened when his empire would not, asks from 1930 the question that has never been answered, only deferred: Will we learn to negotiate with the river, or will we keep building monuments until the delta can bear no more?
The river told him. He tried to tell us. The question remains, and the Ganges Treaty expires in December. The decision window is open. For now.
Zakir Kibria is a Bangladeshi writer, policy analyst and entrepreneur based in Kathmandu, Nepal. His email address is zakir.kibria@gmail.com

item.title: Rethinking development storytelling
item.description:
There is an uncomfortable truth that both the development sector and the entertainment industry in Bangladesh have long avoided confronting openly.
For years, development organizations have invested heavily in documentaries, awareness videos, campaign films, and advocacy content. Many are sincere, technically strong, and socially important, yet most remain confined to workshops, donor reports, institutional platforms, or social media feeds where audiences quickly move on.
They often meet organizational objectives but rarely become part of the wider public. Meanwhile, mainstream cinema and OTT platforms continue focusing largely on romance, crime, thrillers, and urban stories, while the realities of climate frontlines, labour communities, migration, and marginalized lives rarely enter mainstream entertainment with commercial ambition or emotional depth.
Between these two worlds, development storytelling and mainstream entertainment, a quiet wall of misunderstanding has existed for years.
One myth inside the development sector suggests that audiences do not want to engage with stories of marginalized communities unless they are framed as “awareness content.” Another myth within parts of the entertainment industry assumes that films connected to advocacy or development themes inevitably lose commercial appeal and audience engagement.
Cha Gorom , a film by Shankha Dasgupta, co-created by Oxfam and Chorki with support from the European Union, challenged both myths.
Approximately a year and a half ago, Cha Gorom was not even a title but a restless idea and a question that kept disturbing me: Why are Bangladesh’s tea gardens always shown only from the outside?
For decades, films, advertisements, and tourism campaigns have celebrated their beauty yet rarely explored the emotional world of the people who sustain that industry. Over the years, I increasingly felt development storytelling needed a new language. We did not need another documentary that simply informed audiences but rather a commercially viable, emotionally engaging story.
That dream of going out of our zone eventually became the beginning of Cha Gorom . The journey was long and uncertain. We started with research, field visits, discussions, and endless conversations across Dhaka and Sylhet. Oxfam's leadership and the European Union's support encouraged the vision. Gradually, people began joining the journey: Redoan Rony, Jabed Sultan Pias, Masudul Amin Rintu, director Shankha Dasgupta, writer Saifullah Riyad, actors, actress, and eventually the entire cast and crew.
One of the most important turning points was shaping the storytelling approach. Saifullah Riyad understood early that the tea garden could not simply become a backdrop of suffering. From Oxfam, we provided all aspects -- research, community access, history, culture, lived realities, and multiple story angles, while consciously ensuring creative freedom for the filmmakers. Director Shankha Dasgupta then translated those realities beautifully onto the screen.
What audiences connected with most deeply in Cha Gorom was its humanity. Safa Kabir’s portrayal of Irene, a young doctor entering an unfamiliar social world, brought emotional vulnerability, curiosity, and empathy into the narrative. Through Irene, viewers gradually entered a tea garden reality they often overlook in everyday life.
Sarah Jabin Aditi’s Nandini carried a quiet emotional intensity that reflected the layered psychological realities of marginalized women without falling into melodrama. At the same time, Rezwan Pervez’s Robin Chand Murmu emerged as one of the film’s discoveries, bringing grounded realism, philosophy of life, emotional depth, and authenticity to the story. Mithu, played by Partho Sheikh, is charming, flawed, careless, philosophical, and deeply human.
Many viewers later wrote that these characters felt like people they personally knew. That is the true power of emotionally intelligent storytelling.
After its release, social media and mainstream media reactions became revealing. Some viewers appreciated the natural performances. Others highlighted the emotional simplicity of the storytelling.
Journalists, writers, celebrities, masses of people, and cultural commentators wrote positively about how the film balanced entertainment and realism without becoming preachy. Many ordinary viewers mentioned how the film made them rethink tea garden communities for the first time. Many appreciated the approach of Oxfam in the film production.
One viewer wrote something that stayed with me deeply: “This film did not make tea workers look helpless. It made them look human.” That sentence perhaps summarizes the real achievement of the movie. Because dignity matters in storytelling.
Marginalized people should not always appear only as victims waiting to be saved. They laugh, joke, love, dream, flirt, become frustrated, make mistakes, and carry emotional complexity like everyone else. And this is where the development of storytelling must evolve.
The future of advocacy communication cannot remain trapped inside expensive documentary models that only circulate within development circles. If social storytelling wants mass impact, it must learn the language of entertainment, emotion, and popular culture.
The development sector should not automatically view commercialization as the enemy of social impact. In fact, Cha Gorom proved the opposite with stories and further engagements with Robi and Revital Tea.
When audiences voluntarily spend Pohela Boishakh watching a film rooted in tea garden realities, advocacy has already entered public consciousness, living rooms, in a much more profound way than many traditional campaigns achieve.
For me, Cha Gorom journey also revealed another important lesson for Bangladesh’s creative industry. Development organizations should not be treated merely as sponsors or funding sources.
Organizations like Oxfam contribute far beyond money, including knowledge, field access, research, community trust, social understanding, advocacy insight, and human stories that mainstream cinema often cannot access authentically.
Similarly, NGOs must also learn to trust storytelling itself rather than trying to dominate narratives through excessive development and branding anxiety. Audiences do not emotionally connect with logos. They connect with truth, characters, emotion, and humanity.
In Bangladesh there are countless untold stories across climate change, migration, labour, gender, rivers, urbanization, and marginalized communities that can become mainstream cinema if treated with emotional honesty and cinematic intelligence.
Perhaps the real future lies in a new collaborative model where OTT platforms, filmmakers, writers, artists, communities, and development organizations work together.
Personally, Cha Gorom remains one of the most emotional journeys of my life. It has been about breaking a mindset: Breaking the myth that development storytelling cannot be entertaining, breaking the myth that socially meaningful cinema cannot be commercially viable, and breaking the myth that audiences do not care about marginalized communities, and perhaps most importantly, breaking the myth that social impact and mainstream entertainment cannot walk together.
They can. And Cha Gorom has proved it.
Md Sariful Islam is Head of Influencing, Communications, Advocacy and Media, Oxfam in Bangladesh.

item.title: An ode to reforms
item.description:
“To serve or not to serve -- that is the question.
“Whether it’s our karma to witness rape and murder/ Or that the citizens look resigned to bribery and extortion/ The mafia are protected, the honest deserve injustice/ The youth’s dreams of jobs fade/ Are taxpayers bound to pay for inflated projects, suffer power outage?/ Shall depositors get back bank money? And how to raise voice again to bring change/ For the reforms promised miss action and diction/ Thus the masses quietly bear anger/ Then who are there to stand up for the powerless?”
This is a parody inspired by the soliloquy "To be, or not to be, that is the question" in William Shakespeare's Hamlet . The parody covers some of the current Bangladesh issues that are affecting people's life or creating resentment among the conscious segment.
Before the rape and murder of eight-year-old Ramisa in May, there was already a 35% rise in such crimes in April compared to the previous month. The law enforcement personnel had to remain busy dealing with quite a number of extortion cases recently.
Against this backdrop, the government of the Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) has spelt out some development pledges, crafted with jargons in the official 8th five-year plan, that were part of the party's pre-election manifesto to woo the voters.
Creation of 10 million jobs by 2030, turning Bangladesh into a $1 trillion economy by 2034, transformation of agriculture into a high-value sector, increasing investment in public and private sectors, widening export base, higher revenue mobilization, and raising the budget to improve quality of education and healthcare services are some of the major pledges in addition to the critical ones to rebuild systems and institutions.
Attractive, interesting, and timely, albeit ambitious. Yet, these are just promises on paper unless or until they are translated into new reality -- our record of execution of pre-poll pledges is not the best.
Now dealing with the unforeseen reality, will you focus only on addressing the evolving issues you are struggling with to pacify your critics, or concentrate on delivering services as per the manifesto, ignoring the contemporary issues?
In any case or in the pursuit of both the objectives simultaneously, the government needs to prove political willingness and acumen, capacity, determination, due diligence and prudence if it really means business in the delivery of public service.
Furthermore, in the age of social media, people no longer believe in some kind of “steps to be taken” approach. They want to know exactly what you have done or at least how the job would be accomplished.
In an objective scenario, any proponent of democracy would admit that the implementation of what the BNP as a political party promised the people before and after the fall of fascist Hasina regime, especially prior to the parliamentary polls, is simply not possible if the culture of governing the country remains the same.
In the meantime, public expectations and demographic distribution in the political landscape have quietly changed, as exposed slightly during and in the aftermath of the July-August 2024 revolution. Obviously, the state machinery with anti-people orientation and the baggage of about two decades would serve all but benevolent forces and the greater population, who took to the streets in 2024 and elected their representatives from whatever options were available in 2026.
That is the key challenge for Prime Minister Tarique Rahman because his elected regime has been assigned to set in motion democratic governance. Not to go ahead with Hasina's oligarchical rule, but to uphold national interest and ensure maximum public welfare.
A mere soft-spoken image and sincere wish of the incumbent head of the government won't bear fruit if he doesn't have a competent and dedicated team to materialize his words. Well-coordinated teamwork too may not produce the desired results unless the government's legal instruments and institutions work properly.
Unfortunately, the recent rise in incidents of rape, killing, and other forms of crimes and violence demonstrates fearless behaviour of criminals. Any leader would want to improve law and order but to do so, you have to deliver justice and end the culture of impunity. Is it possible to attain overnight what had not been possible without judicial reform, which has been neglected by allowing the executive to interfere with the judiciary?
You cannot guarantee either the citizens' rights or security by giving any signal that bureaucrats, law enforcers, and the men of defense establishment are above law. So, the National Human Rights Commission, if empowered, can excuse the political regime from undue responsibility of dealing with such sensitive issues.
When you've felt it is necessary to create millions of jobs, attain a double-digit gross domestic product (GDP) growth, increase investment manifold, and restore public confidence in banks by reviving them, it is imperative to carry out economic reforms. That is essential for smooth functioning of the economic lifeblood of every stakeholder in society, except plunderers of public resources.
If you are really serious about ending official corruption, bribery, commissions, tender manipulation, and abuse of power in broad daylight, there is no alternative to autonomous functioning of institutions such as a Anti-Corruption Commission.
We also need the media to be liberated from the clutches of external forces so that they may operate to serve public interests and ensure transparency and accountability of the authorities.
Any elected government in developing countries like ours is criticized for deficiency in the so-called good governance, although what we need is fair and equitable management in running the state. The people's representatives will not be able to deliver democratic, welfare-oriented governance as long as they are sabotaged by vested quarters. Hence, massive regulatory reforms are the only option.
A new democratic culture, as people had envisioned throughout the anti-fascist movements, shall not evolve unless we carry out political reforms and the politicians change their mindset. Otherwise, the political parties and politicians in parliament today may also face an existential crisis soon.
These political parties -- the ruling BNP, and opposition Jamaat-e-Islami, and National Citizens' Party (NCP) -- have a historic responsibility to work together for implementing the reforms they agreed upon earlier to build the foundation for uninterrupted democratic governance in the country.
Whether "to be or not to be" is a matter of their political choice.
Khawaza Main Uddin is a journalist. He can be reached at khawaza@gmail.com . Views expressed are the writer’s own.

item.title: Saying no to tobacco
item.description: With May 31 being World No Tobacco Day, Bangladesh must confront a grim reality: Tobacco has long...
